title: Travel & Accommodation
shortSummary: Book a room, check in, and handle travel basics in Hindi with confidence.
culturalNote: India offers everything from grand heritage hotels to simple guesthouses, and a budget lodge is often called a dharamshala or a basic hotel. When you check in, you will usually need to show identification, and foreign visitors fill in a form called a 'C-form'. Hospitality runs deep; the Sanskrit saying 'atithi devo bhava' means 'the guest is like God', and hosts take pride in looking after travellers. Tipping (बख्शीश, bakhsheesh) is appreciated by porters and room staff but is not strictly required. Train travel is a classic Indian experience, and booking ahead through the railways is wise during festival seasons when everything is full. A polite 'kya kamra khaali hai?' (is a room free?) is a useful opener at any guesthouse.

grammar.explanation: To say what you will do, add -unga (man) or -ungi (woman) to the verb stem: rukna (to stay) becomes rukoonga, jaana (to go) becomes jaaunga. The ending carries both the future meaning and the speaker's gender.

examples.native: मैं दो रात रुकूँगा। (main do raat rukoonga.)

grammar.commonMistake: A woman using -unga; she should use the feminine -ungi ending.

grammar.explanation: Kripya (कृपया) means 'please' and is added before a polite -iye verb in formal or written requests. Kripya taxi bulaiye is a courteous 'please call a taxi'; in speech the -iye form alone is already polite.
grammar.commonMistake: Adding kripya to a casual command; pair it with the polite -iye form, not the bare stem.

dialogue.setup: Maya arrives at a guesthouse and speaks with the receptionist, Sunil.

lines.speaker: Sunil
lines.native: नमस्ते! जी हाँ, एक कमरा है।
lines.transliteration: namaste! ji haan, ek kamra hai.
lines.english: Hello! Yes, there is one room.

lines.speaker: Sunil
lines.native: एक हज़ार रुपये, नाश्ता शामिल है।
lines.transliteration: ek hazaar rupaye, naashta shaamil hai.

lines.speaker: Maya
lines.native: ठीक है। मैं दो रात रुकूँगी।
lines.transliteration: theek hai. main do raat rukoongi.

lines.speaker: Sunil
lines.native: बहुत अच्छा। चेक-आउट ग्यारह बजे है।
lines.transliteration: bahut achha. check-out gyaarah baje hai.
lines.english: Very good. Check-out is at eleven.

lines.speaker: Maya
lines.english: Thank you! Please also call a taxi.

quiz.question: How do you ask 'Is a room free?' in Hindi?
quiz.correctAnswer: क्या कमरा खाली है? (kya kamra khaali hai?)
quiz.explanation: Kya turns it into a yes/no question; khaali means 'free/empty'.

quiz.question: A woman saying 'I will stay two nights' should say:
quiz.explanation: Rukoongi is the feminine future; rukoonga is for a man.

quiz.question: What does 'कृपया (kripya)' mean?
quiz.explanation: Kripya is the formal 'please' placed before a polite request.

quiz.question: Arrange: चाहिए / एक / कमरा / मुझे (I need a room)
quiz.correctAnswer: मुझे एक कमरा चाहिए (mujhe ek kamra chahiye)
quiz.explanation: Mujhe, the object ek kamra, then chahiye at the end.
